findings.title: VectorStore.delete() accepts raw filter string (injection risk)
findings.description: The delete method accepts a raw string filter and passes it directly to LanceDB's table.delete(filter). If 'filter' is ever constructed from user input, this is a filter-injection vector (analogous to SQL injection). The JSDoc examples show string-interpolated patterns like "id = 'v1'", encouraging unsafe usage at call sites. Even if callers are currently safe, the API surface is inherently dangerous.
findings.suggestion: Change the API to accept structured filter parameters (e.g., { field: string, op: '=' | 'IN', value: string | string[] }) and build the filter expression internally with proper escaping. Alternatively, provide a safe `deleteById(tableName, id)` helper for the most common case.

findings.title: DocumentStore and VectorStore not wired to any entry point
findings.code_snippet:
export { DocumentStore } from './storage/document-store'
export { VectorStore } from './storage/vector-store'
findings.description: Both DocumentStore and VectorStore are exported from the package index but are not instantiated or consumed by any entry point. The existing setupContextEngine() in apps/stage-tamagotchi/src/main/services/anima/context-engine.ts only wires ScreenshotCapture. Neither store is registered with injeca, connected via IPC, or invoked by any timer, event listener, or handler. This is orphan code -- exported but unreachable from any live execution path.
findings.suggestion: Wire both stores into setupContextEngine() and expose relevant operations via IPC handlers or injeca service providers. At minimum, instantiate DocumentStore and VectorStore in the service setup function so they are reachable.

findings.title: Conversation.role is string but documents a finite union
findings.code_snippet:
/** 'user' | 'assistant' | 'system' */
role: string
findings.description: The JSDoc comment explicitly lists three valid values ('user' | 'assistant' | 'system') but the type is plain `string`. This means the type system cannot catch invalid roles at compile time, and DocumentStore.insertConversation will happily accept role: 'admin' or role: '' with no error. This is a textbook case where the documented invariant should be expressed in the type itself.
findings.suggestion: Define `export type ConversationRole = 'user' | 'assistant' | 'system'` and use it: `role: ConversationRole`. This makes the JSDoc comment redundant (the type IS the documentation) and gives compile-time safety.

findings.title: ContextVector.source is string but documents a finite union
findings.code_snippet:
/** Source of the vector (e.g., 'screenshot', 'conversation', 'document') */
source: string
findings.description: The JSDoc enumerates known source values but the type is `string`, allowing any arbitrary source value. VectorStore.insert has no validation, so misspelled sources (e.g., 'screensht') would silently create unsearchable records.
findings.suggestion: Define `export type VectorSource = 'screenshot' | 'conversation' | 'document'` and use it in both ContextVector and VectorSearchResult. If extensibility is needed, use `VectorSource | (string & {})` pattern.

findings.title: VectorStore: zero error handling on all I/O operations
findings.description: VectorStore has 7 async methods (create, createTable, insert, search, delete, count, close) performing I/O against LanceDB. None have try/catch blocks. Failures such as disk full, corrupted data, missing directory, or invalid table names will propagate as untyped library errors with no context about what operation failed or what input caused it.
findings.suggestion: Wrap each I/O operation in try/catch. Catch errors, add context (operation name, table name, data dir), and re-throw as a typed ContextEngineStorageError. Example: try { const db = await lancedb.connect(dataDir) } catch (err) { throw new StorageError('Failed to connect to vector store', { dataDir, cause: err }) }

findings.title: DocumentStore: zero error handling; constructor leaks DB on failure
findings.description: DocumentStore has 10 methods performing SQLite I/O. None have try/catch blocks. SQLite operations can fail due to disk errors, constraint violations, locked databases, etc. Additionally, if initTables() throws in the constructor, the Database connection opened at line 14 is never closed -- the caller receives an error but has no reference to the partially-constructed DocumentStore to call close(), causing a resource leak.
findings.suggestion: Wrap each method's database operations in try/catch with context. For the constructor specifically: try { this.initTables() } catch (err) { this.db.close(); throw new DocumentStoreError('Failed to initialize tables', { dbPath, cause: err }) }

findings.title: VectorStore.close() nullifies db without use-after-close guard
findings.description: After close() is called, this.db is set to null via unsafe type cast. Any subsequent call to insert(), search(), delete(), count(), or createTable() will throw a TypeError like 'Cannot read properties of null (reading openTable)' with no meaningful error message. There is no guard to check if the store has been closed.
findings.suggestion: Add a private 'closed' boolean flag. Set it to true in close(). Add a guard at the top of each method: if (this.closed) throw new StorageError('VectorStore is closed'). This produces a clear, debuggable error.

findings.title: No app-level integration test proving store wiring
findings.description: The package-level tests for DocumentStore and VectorStore use real SQLite and LanceDB (good). However, there is no integration test at the app level (stage-tamagotchi) proving these stores can be instantiated, wired into injeca, and invoked through the service layer.
findings.suggestion: Add an integration test in apps/stage-tamagotchi that instantiates DocumentStore and VectorStore via the service setup function, performs a round-trip operation (insert + read), and verifies the data flows through.

findings.title: Conversation.role is untyped string instead of union type
findings.code_snippet:
/** 'user' | 'assistant' | 'system' */
  role: string
findings.description: The Conversation interface documents role as 'user' | 'assistant' | 'system' in a JSDoc comment, but the actual type is `string`. Any arbitrary string can be inserted as a role with no compile-time or runtime validation.
findings.suggestion: Change the type to a union: `role: 'user' | 'assistant' | 'system'`. Optionally add runtime validation in insertConversation().

findings.title: Role values documented in comment instead of type union
findings.code_snippet:
/** 'user' | 'assistant' | 'system' */
  role: string
findings.description: The comment enumerates allowed role values but the field type is plain `string`. If new roles are added or existing ones change, the comment will silently become stale.
findings.suggestion: Define a string literal union type and use it as the field type instead of documenting the constraint in a comment.

findings.title: updateIntimacy read-then-write is not atomic
findings.description: updateIntimacy performs a read followed by a write as two separate operations. While better-sqlite3 is synchronous within one process, if multiple connections share the database file, this is a race condition.
findings.suggestion: Use a single atomic SQL statement: `UPDATE intimacy SET level = MAX(0, level + ?) WHERE id = 1`. Then read back the new value.

findings.title: VectorStore.close() nullifies db via unsafe cast
findings.description: After close() is called, this.db is set to null via an unsafe double cast. Any subsequent call will throw a confusing runtime error rather than a clear 'store is closed' error.
findings.suggestion: Make the db property nullable (`private db: lancedb.Connection | null`) and check for null explicitly in each method.

findings.title: No validation on VectorStore.createTable dimension parameter
findings.description: The createTable method accepts a dimension parameter with no validation. A dimension of 0, negative, or extremely large value could cause incorrect behavior or memory issues.
findings.suggestion: Add input validation: `if (!Number.isInteger(dimension) || dimension < 1) throw new Error('Invalid vector dimension')`. Consider an upper bound check as well.

findings.title: No test for operations on non-existent table
findings.code_snippet:
await store.createTable('memories', DIMENSION)
const vectors = [
  makeVector('v1', [0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4], 'first memory'),
]
findings.description: VectorStore operations call this.db.openTable(tableName) which will throw if the table does not exist. No test verifies the error behavior when operating on a non-existent table.
findings.suggestion: Add a test: `it('throws when operating on non-existent table', async () => { await expect(store.search('nonexistent', [0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4], 5)).rejects.toThrow(); })`

findings.title: No test for createTable idempotency
findings.code_snippet: await store.createTable('memories', DIMENSION)
findings.description: VectorStore.createTable contains explicit idempotency logic (checks existing table names and returns early if present). This behavior is never tested.
findings.suggestion: Add a test verifying the second createTable call does not destroy existing data.

findings.title: No test for dimension mismatch in vector search
findings.code_snippet:
const results = await store.search('memories', [0.9, 0.1, 0.0, 0.0], 2)
expect(results).toHaveLength(2)
findings.description: All tests use DIMENSION=4 consistently. No test verifies behavior when a query vector has a different dimension than stored vectors.
findings.suggestion: Add a test for dimension mismatch to document whether LanceDB throws or handles it gracefully.

findings.title: No test for duplicate conversation ID insertion
findings.code_snippet:
const conv: Conversation = {
  id: 'c1',
  role: 'user',
  content: 'Hello Anima',
  createdAt: Date.now(),
}
store.insertConversation(conv)
findings.description: DocumentStore.insertConversation uses INSERT with PRIMARY KEY on id. Inserting a duplicate id will throw a SQLite constraint error. No test verifies this behavior.
findings.suggestion: Add a test verifying duplicate insertion throws, or change to INSERT OR REPLACE and test idempotency.

findings.title: DocumentStore.getIntimacy() assumes row always exists
findings.description: stmt.get() can return undefined if the row was deleted externally. The result is cast and immediately accessed, which would throw an unhandled TypeError.
findings.suggestion: Add a null check: if (!row) throw new DocumentStoreError('Intimacy record not found'). Or use fallback: return row?.level ?? 0.

findings.title: Todo allows completed:true with completedAt:null
findings.description: The Todo interface allows contradictory states: completed=true with completedAt=null, and completed=false with completedAt set. These illegal states are representable by the type.
findings.suggestion: Use a discriminated union: `({ completed: false; completedAt: null } | { completed: true; completedAt: number })`. Or derive completed from completedAt !== null.

findings.title: DocumentStore accepts domain types without input validation
findings.description: DocumentStore boundary methods accept types but perform no validation. Empty id, empty content, negative createdAt, or invalid role will be persisted without error.
findings.suggestion: Create Valibot schemas for Conversation and Todo and validate at the boundary in insertConversation/upsertTodo.

findings.title: No test for empty collections (getTodos, getRecentConversations)
findings.code_snippet:
describe('todos', () => {
  it('inserts and retrieves todos', () => {
findings.description: Neither getTodos() nor getRecentConversations() is tested against an empty database.
findings.suggestion: Add tests verifying empty-state queries return empty arrays.

findings.title: Timestamps use bare number throughout all interfaces
findings.code_snippet:
/** Unix timestamp in milliseconds when this record was created */
createdAt: number
findings.description: All interfaces use bare `number` for timestamps. This prevents compile-time distinction between milliseconds and seconds timestamps.
findings.suggestion: Consider a branded type `type UnixMillis = number & { readonly __brand: 'UnixMillis' }` with a smart constructor. Low priority for walking skeleton.

findings.title: Delete method JSDoc calls LanceDB filter a 'SQL filter expression'
findings.code_snippet:
/**
   * Delete vectors matching the SQL filter expression.
   * Example filter: "id = 'v1'" or "source = 'screenshot'"
   */
findings.description: The JSDoc refers to the filter as a 'SQL filter expression.' LanceDB uses its own syntax which is SQL-like but not SQL. This could mislead maintainers.
findings.suggestion: Change 'SQL filter expression' to 'LanceDB filter expression' or 'filter expression'.

findings.title: Count method JSDoc restates the method name
findings.description: The JSDoc adds no information beyond what the method signature already communicates.
findings.suggestion: Remove the JSDoc or enhance it with useful context (e.g., performance characteristics, inclusion of soft-deleted rows).

findings.title: Multiple individual type assertions in search result mapping
findings.code_snippet:
return results.map(row => ({
  id: row.id as string,
  source: row.source as string,
  content: row.content as string,
  createdAt: row.createdAt as number,
  _distance: row._distance as number,
}))
findings.description: Five individual type assertions on each field. A single typed cast on the row would be clearer and consolidate the type boundary.
findings.suggestion: Replace with: `return results.map(row => row as unknown as VectorSearchResult)` -- consolidates 5 assertions to 1 cast.

findings.title: Double cast through unknown to null the db reference
findings.code_snippet: this.db = null as unknown as lancedb.Connection
findings.description: The close() method uses a double cast escape hatch to null the db. This hides the fact that the field could be null after close().
findings.suggestion: Make the field nullable: `private db: lancedb.Connection | null` and set `this.db = null` directly.

findings.title: Close method comment references 'future compatibility' (rot risk)
findings.description: The comment mentions 'future compatibility' as justification. This forward-looking claim may rot if LanceDB never adds a close method.
findings.suggestion: Rephrase to: 'This method exists for interface symmetry so callers can consistently clean up resources.' Drop the speculative framing.

findings.title: Inconsistent result type handling between stores
findings.code_snippet: return stmt.all(limit) as Conversation[]
findings.description: getRecentConversations directly casts the result while getTodos uses manual mapping for boolean conversion. The approaches are inconsistent.
findings.suggestion: Add a brief inline comment explaining why no mapping is needed for Conversation (all columns alias directly), making the contrast with getTodos intentional.

positive_observations: Tests use real SQLite and LanceDB databases with temp directories -- no mocks or in-memory fakes for storage boundaries (Dev/Prod Parity).
Persistence tests verify data survives across reconnect (close + reopen), proving real durability.
SQL uses parameterized queries throughout DocumentStore (INSERT/SELECT/UPDATE all use ? placeholders) -- no SQL injection in document store.
WAL mode enabled for SQLite, demonstrating awareness of concurrent read/write performance.
Clean separation between types, storage implementations, and package exports.
DocumentStore schema uses proper constraints (PRIMARY KEY, NOT NULL, CHECK) for data integrity.
Intimacy table uses CHECK (id = 1) constraint to enforce singleton row at the database level.
VectorStore uses private constructor + static factory pattern, properly encapsulating the LanceDB connection.
VectorStore.search() correctly handles empty tables by checking rowCount before searching.
VectorSearchResult is a separate type from ContextVector, avoiding leaking internal vector data to search consumers.
Proper test isolation via temp directories with cleanup in afterEach prevents cross-test contamination.
VectorStore tests verify search result ordering by distance, not just presence -- testing actual semantic behavior.
DocumentStore intimacy tests cover the floor-at-zero boundary condition (negative delta beyond zero).
All functions are short (under 15 lines) with low cyclomatic complexity.
Consistent use of guard clauses and early returns (createTable existing check, search empty table check).
JSDoc is consistently applied to all exported classes, methods, and interface fields.
No forbidden patterns (TODO, FIXME, HACK, PLACEHOLDER, TEMP) found in any of the files.
